skill.name: cqrs-implementation
skill.description: Guides implementation of Command Query Responsibility Segregation with command models, query models, projections, and consistency patterns.
skill.summary: Build CQRS systems with separate write models, read models, event projections, and consistency handling.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported shell execution, weak cryptography, network, and reconnaissance patterns. Review found these are Markdown fences, architecture text, database query examples, and documentation links, with no executable skill code or prompt injection evidence. The remaining risk is low because users may adapt sample SQL and API patterns without adding their own validation and authorization.

low_findings.title: Static Shell Execution Findings Are Markdown Delimiters
low_findings.description: The external command findings are caused by Markdown code fences and inline resource paths, not executable Ruby or shell code. The skill contains documentation and code samples only, so these detections are false positives.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are Markdown fences or inline Markdown paths. No command runner, shell invocation, or executable script is present at these locations.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are Text Matches
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography findings match ordinary CQRS wording and descending sort direction examples. No cryptographic API, cipher selection, password hashing, or security-sensitive encoding was found at the flagged lines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content is descriptive prose or SQL sort order text. The surrounding context contains no crypto library use or secret handling.

low_findings.title: Network Findings Are Database Calls And Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The fetch finding is an async database fetch call inside a sample query handler, not a browser Fetch API call. The hardcoded URLs point to public CQRS reference documentation and do not transmit data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The database fetch call uses a local connection object, and the only URLs are Markdown references. I found no outbound request code or data exfiltration path.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Findings Are Identifier And SQL Text Matches
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance findings are false positives from identifiers, UUID generation, SQL field names, and command dispatch examples. No host enumeration, environment probing, process listing, or filesystem inspection behavior was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are normal application identifiers and SQL snippets in a CQRS example. They do not invoke discovery commands or collect system metadata.

low_findings.title: Sample SQL Requires Careful Adaptation
low_findings.description: The query examples use parameter binding for user values, but they also assemble SQL text for optional clauses. This is acceptable in the shown context, yet users should keep any future dynamic clauses allowlisted and parameterized.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The current sample builds SQL from controlled fragments and binds values. The risk is guidance quality rather than a confirmed vulnerability in executable skill code.

content.value_statement: Teams often struggle to scale reads and writes with one shared model. This skill provides CQRS structures, templates, and checks for commands, queries, projections, and consistency.
actual_capabilities: Explains when CQRS fits and when simpler CRUD is better.
Defines command, command handler, query, and query handler responsibilities.
Provides Python-style templates for buses, handlers, and FastAPI endpoints.
Shows read model projection and rebuild patterns for event-driven systems.
Covers eventual consistency checks and read-your-writes behavior.
Lists implementation practices for event versioning and read model design.
limitations: It provides guidance and sample patterns, not a complete production framework.
Examples require adaptation for authentication, authorization, validation, and observability.
It assumes teams can operate separate read and write data models.
It is not suited for simple CRUD domains that need immediate consistency everywhere.

use_cases.title: Separate Read And Write Models
use_cases.description: Design distinct command and query models for a growing backend with different read and write needs.

use_cases.title: Plan Event-Driven Projections
use_cases.description: Define projection rebuild, checkpoint, and synchronization behavior for event-sourced services.

use_cases.title: Evaluate CQRS Fit
use_cases.description: Compare CQRS against simpler CRUD before committing to extra operational complexity.

prompt_templates.title: Check CQRS Fit
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner assessment for a service design.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this service description and tell me if CQRS is justified. Identify read workloads, write workloads, consistency needs, and simpler alternatives.

prompt_templates.title: Design Command And Query Models
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate modeling for a bounded context.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create CQRS command models, query models, handlers, and validation rules for this domain. Keep read and write responsibilities separate.

prompt_templates.title: Build Projection Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced planning for read model synchronization.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design read model projections for these domain events. Include checkpoints, rebuild behavior, failure handling, and expected consistency lag.

prompt_templates.title: Review Production Readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this CQRS design for production readiness. Cover consistency guarantees, recovery, observability, schema evolution, authorization, and operational risks.

output_examples.input: A retail order service needs fast customer order history and strict validation for order changes.
output: A recommended split between order commands and customer order queries.
A list of command handlers for create, add item, and cancel operations.
A read model projection plan optimized for customer order history.

output_examples.input: An event-sourced billing system needs rebuilt reporting views after schema changes.
output: A projection rebuild workflow with checkpoint reset and replay steps.
Failure handling guidance for skipped or retried events.
Consistency checks that show when reporting views are current.

output_examples.input: A team wants CQRS for a small admin settings service.
output: A recommendation to avoid CQRS unless read and write complexity grows.
A simpler CRUD alternative with validation and audit logging.
Criteria that would justify revisiting CQRS later.

best_practices: Start with workload analysis before creating separate models.
Keep command validation close to command handlers and domain rules.
Measure projection lag and define recovery steps before launch.
anti_patterns: Applying CQRS to simple CRUD screens without scale or model complexity.
Sharing one schema between read and write sides after claiming separation.
Ignoring eventual consistency behavior in user-facing workflows.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps design CQRS systems with commands, queries, read models, projections, and consistency handling.

faq.question: Does it generate a complete application?
faq.answer: No. It provides patterns and templates that must be adapted to your framework and production requirements.

faq.question: When should I avoid CQRS?
faq.answer: Avoid it when the domain is simple, CRUD is enough, or immediate consistency is required everywhere.

faq.question: Does it cover event sourcing?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes event projection, read model synchronization, and projection rebuild guidance.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude, Codex, or Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: What security review is still needed?
faq.answer: Review any adapted code for authorization, input validation, SQL safety, observability, and operational controls.
